skill.name: global-commenting
skill.description: Guides assistants to write minimal, durable code comments that explain intent, complex logic, and business rules without repeating obvious code behavior.
skill.summary: Create concise, evergreen code comments that explain why code exists.
skill.author: EIS-ITS

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged path traversal sequences and weak cryptography patterns in SKILL.md. The path traversal finding is a real packaging and filesystem concern because the skill points outside its own directory, while the weak cryptography findings are false positives with no cryptographic code present.

medium_findings.title: External Relative Documentation Reference
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the assistant to refer to a Markdown file using ../../../ traversal. This can make the skill depend on files outside its package and may cause an assistant to read unintended repository paths if the referenced file exists.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The traversal sequence is directly visible in the referenced Markdown link. The risk is medium because it is a documentation reference, not executable code or confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Is False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at SKILL.md lines 3 and 24. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, hashing calls, encoded payloads, or instructions to use weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose describing code comments and coding standards. There is no semantic evidence of cryptographic behavior in the available file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Directory Traversal Sequence In Markdown Link
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses ../../../ in a documentation link. This is not executable by itself, but it can prompt filesystem access outside the skill package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal sequence is present in the link target. The surrounding context indicates documentation indirection rather than malware.

content.value_statement: Code comments often become noisy, stale, or focused on obvious behavior. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward concise comments that explain intent.
actual_capabilities: Helps decide when code needs explanatory comments.
Guides comments toward intent, tradeoffs, and business logic.
Supports docstrings and function documentation decisions.
Encourages clearer naming and structure before adding comments.
Helps review and remove stale or temporary comments.
limitations: The packaged skill contains only a brief pointer to a separate standard.
It does not provide language-specific comment syntax examples.
It cannot verify whether comments match runtime behavior by itself.
It may need project context to judge business logic comments.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Request Comments
use_cases.description: Check whether new comments explain intent and remove notes about temporary changes.

use_cases.title: Document Complex Logic
use_cases.description: Add concise comments around algorithms, edge cases, or business rules that are hard to infer.

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Documentation
use_cases.description: Update docstrings and inline comments so they stay useful after future refactors.

prompt_templates.title: Check Comment Need
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review before adding a comment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this function and tell me whether it needs a comment. Prefer clearer naming or structure before adding one.

prompt_templates.title: Write Intent Comments
prompt_templates.scenario: Add useful comments to complex code
prompt_templates.prompt: Add minimal comments to this code. Focus on why decisions exist, not what each line does.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Stale Comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these comments for stale, temporary, or change-history language. Rewrite only the comments that should remain.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Documentation Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this module for comment quality. Identify missing intent, redundant comments, weak docstrings, and places where code should be clearer.

output_examples.input: A function has several comments explaining each assignment.
output_examples.output: Most comments are redundant. Rename intermediate variables and keep one comment explaining the non-obvious business rule.

output_examples.input: A payment eligibility rule includes a regulatory exception.
output_examples.output: Add a short comment above the exception explaining the policy reason and the condition it protects.

output_examples.input: A docstring describes parameters but omits side effects.
output_examples.output: Keep the parameter summary concise and add one sentence describing the important side effect.

best_practices: Ask for comments only after the code structure is understandable.
Provide domain context when business rules are not obvious.
Review generated comments for accuracy before committing them.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to add comments to every line.
Do not use comments to record recent fixes or temporary history.
Do not keep a comment when clearer code can replace it.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps assistants write concise code comments that explain intent, complex logic, and business rules.

faq.question: Does it generate full documentation sites?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on code comments, docstrings, and comment review decisions.

faq.question: Can it work with any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is language-neutral and applies to source files that may need comments.

faq.question: Will it add more comments automatically?
faq.answer: No. It encourages comments only when they improve understanding beyond clear code.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill references a file outside its package with a traversal path. No malicious code was found.

faq.question: What should publishers fix before release?
faq.answer: They should include the referenced commenting standard inside the skill package or replace the link with local guidance.
